approach: test-only binding job: no spec or suite content change, every target scenario already exists verbatim and stays frozen, node status stays implemented — so no spec gate is invoked, following the complete-mux-scenario-coverage precedent

the issue's counts are stale (45/72 filed, 48/77 measured on the rebased tree) and its causal claim is half wrong: worktree.test.ts carries no spec node wrapper at all, so the structural cause the issue ruled out is in play alongside the per-test title cause

the CR's one real risk is a rename-induced over-claim — a retitle makes a test claim its scenario, and a false bind is worse than an unbound one because unbound gets hand-judged while a false bind gets trusted; each retitle is proven by mutating the scenario's subject rather than by reading

title: The two key-refusal scenarios cannot be proven by a mocked exec, and no integration tier covers them
note: Two frozen scenarios at the pane node assert what a BACKEND does with a token cyber-mux forwards: herdr refusing an unsupported key, and tmux typing an unknown token as literal characters because it has no way to refuse. Both are facts about the real binary. Every adapter test fakes the command runner and can only record argv, so a mocked test can pin the forwarding half and is structurally incapable of pinning the refusal half — the existing herdr test says so in its own comment. Deliberately left UNBOUND by this CR rather than retitled: a bind here would claim a refusal nothing ran. Closing them needs an opt-in integration tier driving a real herdr/tmux, which is a different kind of work than a binding job.

title: Three worktree scenarios state their Given in env terms the tests never exercise
note: `worktree add --at workspace groups the worktree where the backend binds` and the two --label scenarios are Scenario Outlines whose Givens name $HERDR_ENV/$TMUX, but every candidate test passes an adapter OBJECT directly or selects via $CYBER_MUX, so the env-resolution the Given describes is never the thing under test; their Examples rows are also split across per-backend tests, one row each, so no single leaf carries the outline. Left unbound rather than bound to a one-row body claiming all of them. The open question is whether these outlines should be re-stated at the layer that actually resolves env, or the tests moved up to the CLI — a spec question, not a test-titling one, which is why this CR did not settle it.

title: Two worktree scenarios are each half-covered: `list and remove outside a multiplexer`, and `removal is never delegated to the backend`
note: The first says list OR remove answers with no multiplexer; both candidate tests cover list only, and no test drives a normal remove with no adapter. The second asserts removal is never delegated, but its Given — a backend with a worktree-removal primitive of its own — is never set up, and nothing asserts such a primitive goes uncalled; the property holds by construction today because the session capability interface exposes no remove at all, which is why nothing tests it. Both need a new assertion rather than a retitle, and both are cheap. Left unbound because a retitle would have claimed the missing half.

title: The scenario bridge cannot distinguish a scenario with no test from one whose test title near-misses by a character
note: One scenario at the pane node was reported UNBOUND for a reason that had nothing to do with the implementation-voiced titles the source issue diagnosed: its test title was already the scenario name verbatim except for a curly apostrophe where the suite has a straight one. The bridge matches the leaf title verbatim, so the two are simply different keys, and the report shows the scenario as UNBOUND while the very same string appears in the EXTRA list — the diagnosis is sitting in the output, but only to a reader who thinks to compare the two lists character by character. That is a silent trap for exactly the maintenance this bridge invites, and the same class of failure as the unparseable-suite bug already filed for this project: a real signal rendered invisible. Proposal: have the bridge diagnose near-misses, reporting an UNBOUND scenario whose normalized form matches an EXTRA key as a probable title mismatch rather than as absent coverage. Cheap, and it turns a character hunt into a printed answer.
